name: Collecting Data — Ask, Tally, Discover
whatIsIt: Data is information you COLLECT to answer a question: ask everyone the same clear question, record answers with TALLY marks (bundle the fifth across: ||||), total them into a table, then graph and read the story. Question → collect → tally → table → graph → answer!
whyNeeded: Guessing says 'I think most kids like cricket'; DATA proves it. Shops decide what to stock, schools plan events, doctors track health — all by collecting data first. Whoever collects carefully gets answers they can trust (and spots others' shaky claims!).
realLifeUses: Class party planning: survey which snack to order
Weather diary: sunny, cloudy, rainy days this month
Shop keeping: which flavour sells most, to restock right

vocabulary.meaning: Collected pieces of information — answers, counts, measurements.
Quick counting sticks: |||| = 4, the 5th crosses the bundle = 5.
Asking many people the SAME question and recording every answer.

story.title: Choosing the Festival Snack Fairly
story.text: The class must pick ONE snack for the festival. Ravi shouts 'Jalebi!' but the teacher smiles: 'Let's decide fairly — with DATA.' They survey everyone, tally the answers (laddu 9, samosa 7, jalebi 4), and draw a graph. 'Laddu wins — decided by the data, not by the loudest voice!' Meena grins at Ravi.
story.extractedProblem: How should the class decide the festival snack fairly?
story.answerInStory: Survey everyone, tally the answers (laddu 9, samosa 7, jalebi 4), graph — laddu wins by data, not by Ravi!

visual.caption: From tally slate to bar graph: |||| |||| → 9, |||| || → 7, |||| → 4. The graph ends the argument!

teachingGallery.title: Tally marks — count in fives
teachingGallery.note: Draw 4 lines, then the 5th crosses them like a gate. Count the gates in fives.

examples.caption: Four lines + one crossing = a bundle of 5.
4 stays open; the 5th closes the gate.
Turn tallies into a bar graph.

standardMethod.summary: Question → collect → tally → table → graph → answer.
steps: Fix ONE clear question with clear choices ('Which snack: laddu / samosa / jalebi?').
Ask everyone; record each answer instantly as a tally stick (5th crosses the bundle!).
Total the tallies into a table.
Draw the graph and READ it: winner, loser, total, gaps.

alternateMethods.name: Observation counting
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When you WATCH instead of asking (traffic, birds, weather).
steps: Decide what counts (red cars? crows?) and for how long.
Tally every sighting in the moment — never trust memory.
Total and graph, same as a survey.

workedExamples.problem: Anu's travel survey tallies: walk |||| |||| , bus |||| ||, cycle |||| . Turn the tallies into numbers.
steps: cycle: one bundle = 5. (Table done — ready to graph!)

workedExamples.problem: Ravi surveyed 'Is cricket the best sport? Yes or yes?!' and reports 100% say yes. What went wrong before he even counted?
steps: The QUESTION was broken — it allowed only one answer!
Fair data needs a fair question with real choices: 'Which sport do you like most: cricket / football / kho-kho?'
Rule: fix the question first; crooked questions make crooked data.
workedExamples.answer: The question forced the answer — fair choices first!

trickPractice.intro: Each crossed gate is 5. Count the gates by fives, then add the loose marks.

questions.q: Count the tally: 1 closed gate(s) of 5 and 3 loose mark(s). Total?
Count the tally: 2 closed gate(s) of 5 and 0 loose mark(s). Total?
Count the tally: 1 closed gate(s) of 5 and 2 loose mark(s). Total?
Count the tally: 2 closed gate(s) of 5 and 4 loose mark(s). Total?
Count the tally: 0 closed gate(s) of 5 and 4 loose mark(s). Total?
Count the tally: 3 closed gate(s) of 5 and 1 loose mark(s). Total?

commonMistakes.mistake: Asking everyone first and trying to remember the answers afterwards.
commonMistakes.fix: Memory drops data! Tally EACH answer the second you hear it — the slate remembers so you don't have to.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing five separate sticks ||||| instead of crossing the bundle, then miscounting piles of sticks.
commonMistakes.fix: The 5th stick LIES ACROSS its four friends: ||||. Bundles of 5 count fast: 5, 10, 15... That's the whole point of tallying!

commonMistakes.mistake: Asking a question that pushes one answer ('Laddus are the best, right?').
commonMistakes.fix: Ask neutral questions with equal choices. Data's job is to SURPRISE you if you're wrong — a loaded question can't.

easy.q: A tally shows |||| |||| || . What number is that?
When should you record each survey answer?
hintLadder: What happens to answers you 'save for later'?

medium.q: Snack votes: laddu 9, samosa 7, jalebi 4. How many people were surveyed in total?

options.label: Which fruit do you like most: mango, banana, or apple?
Mango is everyone's favourite, isn't it?

medium.answer: Which fruit do you like most: mango, banana, or apple?
hintLadder: A fair question offers real choices.

challenge.q: Weather diary tallies — sunny: |||| |||| |||| (3 bundles), rainy: |||| (1 bundle), cloudy: |||| |||| (2 bundles). How many days were recorded altogether?
hintLadder: Each crossed bundle = 5. Count bundles per weather type.
Sunny 3 bundles = 15, rainy 1 bundle = 5, cloudy 2 bundles = 10.
challenge.explain: Bundles of 5 count fast: 15 + 5 + 10 = 30 days — a whole month of weather!

challenge.q: Robo wants to know which vehicles pass the school most. Should he SURVEY or OBSERVE?

options.label: Observe — watch and tally the vehicles themselves
Survey the vehicles with a questionnaire

challenge.answer: Observe — watch and tally the vehicles themselves
hintLadder: Some data is WATCHED, not asked.

questions.q: The correct order of a data project is…

options.label: Graph first, then find data to match

hintLadder: What must exist before you can collect?

questions.q: Why does the 5th tally stick cross the bundle?

options.label: So finished bundles of 5 can be counted fast: 5, 10, 15…

questions.answer: So finished bundles of 5 can be counted fast: 5, 10, 15…

questions.q: Votes were laddu 9, samosa 7, jalebi 4. How many MORE chose laddu than jalebi?

teachBackPrompt: Run the Great Snack Vote at home! Ask everyone one fair question with 3 choices, tally live, total, graph, and announce the result — then explain to your family why 'I asked myself and I agree' isn't data.

revisionCard.summary: Data project: fair QUESTION → tally EVERY answer instantly (5th stick crosses: |||| = 5) → table → graph → read. Loaded questions make crooked data. Bundles of 5 count fast. Data replaces 'I think' with 'we know'!

gameMission.title: The Grove Census
gameMission.brief: The Newsroom is running the first-ever Grove Census! Write fair questions, tally the stampede of answers without dropping any, and expose Ravi's 'surveys of himself' before they reach print.

realLifeProject: Be a window scientist: pick a 15-minute slot and tally something you can see (birds, vehicle types, people with umbrellas). Table it, graph it, and share one surprising discovery with your family.
